nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and sheltered seaward reefs with mixed sand, rubble, and coral; occurring singly, in pairs, or in small female groups. Reported from roughly 1–30 m.

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed (5+ cm) for nocturnal burying
well-established live rock with abundant microfauna

diet.notes: In the wild, the Vermiculate Leopard Wrasse has been documented feeding on small invertebrates picked from the substratum, including foraminiferans, small crustaceans, and other hard-shelled prey. In captivity it tends to require a mature reef system with copious copepod and amphipod populations to graze continuously; over time most specimens accept vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, brine shrimp, and other small meaty foods, and some take high-quality marine flake and pellets. Frequent small feedings — commonly cited as two to three per day — are generally advised, and a connected refugium tends to help keep microfauna replenished.

avoidWith: fish that compete for sand-bed territory
multiple males of the same species in standard-sized tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Jon Hanson; 1024x827. Three individuals together in-situ, sharp and vibrant, clearly showing the diagnostic red-orange spotted body, black flank patch with bright blue vermiculations, and green reticulated face; group shot ideal for this haremic/shoaling species.

Elisabeth Morcel; 1200x857. Tack-sharp single specimen, whole fish in frame, densely blue-white spotted red body with black blue-reticulated flank patch and green vermiculated head; textbook diagnostic coloration.
Diego Delso; 4422x2948. Sharp in-situ single fish with clean bokeh background, gold-orange anterior grading to spotted body with black blue-vermiculated patch; adds coloration variety.

summary: The Vermiculate Leopard Wrasse is a Western Indian Ocean sand-bed species generally considered expert-only, owing to high shipping mortality, dependence on mature live-rock microfauna, and the need for a deep fine sand bed.
careNotes: The Vermiculate Leopard Wrasse is widely regarded as expert-only and is best suited to keepers with mature, microfauna-rich reef systems. Females show the iconic dark body with bright wavy blue-green vermiculations that give the species its common name; terminal males are more orangish-red with green-mottled patterning typical of the genus. Initial shipping mortality has been reported as high, with new arrivals often stressed and prone to refusing prepared foods for days to weeks; slow, low-light acclimation in a quiet quarantine is generally advised. Long-term success is commonly tied to a well-established tank with copious copepod and amphipod populations, ideally backed by a connected refugium. A deep, fine sand bed of at least 5 cm (preferably more) is generally considered essential, as individuals sleep buried at night and dive into the sand when startled. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised — these fish have been known to launch from open tanks when spooked. Tends to settle best when introduced before potentially aggressive tankmates such as tangs, angelfish, hawkfish, or damsels. As with the rest of the genus, Macropharyngodon bipartitus is a protogynous hermaphrodite: most individuals start as females and the dominant fish transitions to male; multiple males in a standard-sized aquarium tend to fight, so most keepers maintain a harem of one male with several females, or a group of three or more juvenile females introduced simultaneously. Note that the trade name 'Black Leopard Wrasse' is also applied to Macropharyngodon kuiteri (Kuiter's Leopard Wrasse, in reference to its black opercular spot) and occasionally to the female phase of this species; identification by collection locality (Western Indian Ocean for bipartitus, Western Pacific/Australia for kuiteri) is generally more reliable than common name.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Vermiculate Leopard Wrasse has not been documented in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: AquariumDomain
primary.notes: Designated primary (LiveAquaria product URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/vermiculate-leopard-wrasse returned HTTP 404 at access). AquariumDomain provided complete husbandry parameter table: min tank 55 gal (~208 L), 72–80 °F (22.2–26.7 °C), pH 8.1–8.4, sg 1.020–1.025 (stable 1.021–1.023 recommended), dKH 8–12, max 5 in (~12.7 cm), expert care, peaceful, reef-safe, carnivore needing live zooplankton, 3+ in sand bed required, sealed lid required, one male with 4–6 females in 110+ gal.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked max length (13.0 cm TL), depth range (?–30 m), preferred temperature (24.9–29.1 °C, mean 27.5 °C — averaged with AquariumDomain 22.2–26.7 °C to a final 23–27 °C range), Western Indian Ocean distribution (Zanzibar to Mozambique, plus Aldabra, Seychelles, Réunion, Mauritius, KwaZulu-Natal, Chagos, southern Oman, Rodrigues, Yemen), family Labridae / order Eupercaria, diet (invertebrates picked from substratum, trophic level 3.5 ±0.37), and reproductive mode (oviparous, distinct pairing). FishBase confirms burrowing-in-sand defensive behavior; sex-change mode not stated explicitly but genus-wide protogyny is taken as default.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked min tank size (50 gal — averaged with AquariumDomain 55 gal and biased upward to 300 L given jumping risk and need for harem swimming space), adult size (5 in / ~12.7 cm), expert care, peaceful temperament, carnivore diet, reef-safe, and known jumping behavior requiring a covered tank. Native range listed as 'Indian Ocean, Red Sea, Africa' — note FishBase distribution does NOT include the Red Sea proper, and the broader range listing is treated as approximate.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. The species is oviparous, spawning in distinct pairs. Like most members of the wrasse family (Labridae), Macropharyngodon bipartitus is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning most individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish transitions to male.
